I'm also checking the agent API to understand how to configure iteration limits and other parameters properly with the ChatClientAgent and function factory approach.

Writing the implementation... I'm setting up the chat client with the OpenAI API and creating a ChatClientAgent that will orchestrate the agent loop with the tools I defined. The agent should automatically handle function invocation when the model decides to use one of the tools. For the search function, I'll need to implement it with either a real API or simulated results since we don't have a search service key available.

Actually, I can use DuckDuckGo's free instant answer API to fetch real search results without needing an API key. I'll implement a SearchWeb function that calls that endpoint and returns actual results, then add a FetchUrl function to retrieve content from specific URLs.

Implementing the search tools... Writing the C# implementation... Writing the web utilities...

Now I'm thinking about the DuckDuckGo API—it gives quick answers through fields like Abstract and RelatedTopics, which is useful for instant results, but it doesn't provide full search results like a traditional search engine would. I should probably look at a different approach that gives me more comprehensive search capabilities.

Actually, the instant answer API is fine for this—it returns clean JSON without needing HTML parsing, and even though the results are limited, it'll work well enough to show how the agent loop functions. The key is getting the core mechanism right. I'm looking at the response structure with fields like Abstract, AbstractURL, RelatedTopics, and Results, and I'll parse that into a nicely formatted output. I'm going to stick with the instant answer API since it returns clean JSON and keeps the demo focused on showcasing the agent framework rather than building a production search tool. For the `FetchWebPage` tool, I'll use `HttpClient` to grab the page content and strip out HTML tags to return just the text, keeping the output bounded so it doesn't bloat the context. I also need to make sure the `ChatClientAgent` has a `FunctionInvokingChatClient` in the pipeline to actually execute the tools.

Looking at the API docs, `ChatClientAgent` automatically orchestrates the agent loop with tool invocation built in, and it applies default decorators like `FunctionInvokingChatClient` unless I explicitly skip them with `UseProvidedChatClientAsIs`. Now I'll structure the implementation with a main entry point, separate tool classes for web search and note-taking, and get everything wired up.

Writing the implementation... Setting up the research agent with instructions that define how it should search the web, save findings, and synthesize research into a comprehensive summary, then configuring it with the chat client and tools I've already defined.

Adding logging middleware... I'm going to add console logging directly in the tool methods themselves to provide visual feedback as the agent executes each action. Now I'm setting up the web search tool with an HTTP client and configuring the necessary headers for making requests.

Implementing the search logic... Building the markdown output... Writing the fetch function... Writing HTML parsing utilities... I'm building out the method to retrieve all saved notes, which formats them nicely with a StringBuilder and handles the case where no notes exist yet. Now I'm thinking about the chat options for this research notebook tool.

Looking at the ChatClientAgent constructor, I need to figure out how to pass ChatOptions like temperature and max tokens. The standard constructor doesn't have a ChatOptions parameter, but there's an overload that takes ChatClientAgentOptions instead, which should give me more control over the configuration. I'm realizing I need to use the options-based constructor to set the temperature through ChatOptions, so I'll configure the agent with the ChatClientAgentOptions object that includes the temperature and tools settings.

Now I'm checking where the instructions parameter goes—it looks like ChatClientAgentOptions might have an Instructions property, but I need to verify that since the first constructor takes instructions as a separate parameter. I can set the ChatOptions directly on the agent after construction, which gives me a cleaner way to configure temperature and max tokens without needing to pass them through run options each time. I need to figure out whether to set instructions via the agent's constructor parameter or through `ChatOptions.Instructions`, since they might serve different purposes—the former being a system prompt and the latter potentially request-specific instructions. I'll go with the constructor's `instructions` parameter for the system prompt and skip the `ChatOptions` approach.

For temperature control, I'm setting up a `ChatClientAgentRunOptions` object with a `ChatOptions` property to configure the temperature to 0.2. Let me verify that `ChatClientAgentRunOptions` actually exists and inherits from `AgentRunOptions` as expected.

Looking at the API, I can see it does exist and includes a `ChatOptions` property that lets me override defaults per run, plus a `ChatClientFactory` for custom client configuration. I should make sure I'm using the right namespace—`Microsoft.Agents.AI`—and then write out the final implementation.

I'm also thinking about iteration limits since the guidance emphasizes capping agentic loops, but I'm not seeing a `MaximumIterations` property on `AgentRunOptions` yet. Let me check what `FunctionInvokingChatClient` offers...

It has `MaximumIterationsPerRequest`, which `ChatClientAgent` probably uses internally through its decorators. I could configure this using a builder pattern to set a reasonable cap like 10 iterations, but I need to be careful about double invocation if `ChatClientAgent` already wraps the client with its own `FunctionInvokingChatClient`.

Now I'm finishing the agent setup by getting the research topic from command-line arguments or using a default, then I'll run the agent to process the topic.
